2019 · FAccT · Benchmarks & Evals

Model Cards for Model Reporting

A proposed standard document — model details, intended use, factors, metrics, evaluation/training data, quantitative analyses broken out by demographic and intersectional subgroup, ethical considerations, caveats — that a trained model should ship alongside its release, modeled on the datasheet that already accompanies every electronics component.

Margaret Mitchell, Simone Wu, Andrew Zaldivar, Parker Barnes, Lucy Vasserman, Ben Hutchinson, Elena Spitzer, Inioluwa Deborah Raji, Timnit Gebru
venue FAccT citations 2,928 topic Benchmarks & Evals source ↗
// citation count as of 2026-07-19 (Semantic Scholar)
// the gap

What problem is this solving?

By late 2018, trained machine learning models were already making decisions with real consequences in law enforcement, medicine, education, and employment, yet — in the authors' own words — "there are no standardized documentation procedures to communicate the performance characteristics" of a released model. That silence wasn't hypothetical: researchers had already found systematic biases in commercial systems used for face detection and tracking, attribute detection, criminal justice, and toxic-comment detection, and in nearly every case the errors surfaced only after the model was already deployed and affected users spoke up. The paper's own headline example is Joy Buolamwini and Timnit Gebru's Gender Shades audit (co-authored by this paper's last author), which found commercial gender-classification APIs erring far more often on darker-skinned women than on lighter-skinned men — a gap invisible to any single aggregate accuracy number, because aggregate accuracy is exactly what a benchmark leaderboard reports.

A parallel, very recent proposal made the same argument one layer down the stack: Gebru et al.'s Datasheets for Datasets, posted to arXiv just seven months earlier, argued that no standard document existed for what a training dataset contains or was built for. Mitchell et al. make the mirror-image case for the trained model itself — even a perfectly documented dataset says nothing about how the resulting model actually behaves once evaluated across the demographic, phenotypic, and environmental conditions it will be deployed into. Without a standard place to report that, disaggregated evaluation results either don't get computed at all, or get computed once internally and never surface publicly where an affected user, a policymaker, or a downstream integrator could see them.

// how it works

The method

Model cards borrow their form from an engineering discipline outside machine learning entirely: every component sold in the electronics industry ships with a datasheet specifying its tested operating characteristics, and the authors explicitly position model cards as the ML equivalent — also likening the idea to the TRIPOD statement, an existing reporting standard for prediction models in medicine. A model card is organized into nine sections that walk from what the model is to how well it actually performs where it matters (Figure 1): Model Details (developer, date, version, architecture, training algorithm, license, contact); Intended Use (primary use cases, primary users, and explicitly out-of-scope uses); Factors (the demographic, phenotypic, environmental, or technical groups performance should be sliced by — e.g., race, gender, Fitzpatrick skin type, camera hardware, lighting/humidity); Metrics (which performance measures, at which decision thresholds, with what uncertainty estimates); Evaluation Data and Training Data (what the model was tested and trained on, and why); Quantitative Analyses (unitary results per single factor, plus intersectional results for factor combinations); Ethical Considerations; and Caveats and Recommendations.

The paper's central methodological demand is that Quantitative Analyses be disaggregated — not just reported for "all," but broken out per relevant Factor and per intersection of Factors, since a model can look fair in aggregate while badly failing one intersectional subgroup. For binary classifiers the authors recommend four metrics computable directly from the confusion matrix, noting that parity across subgroups on different pairs of them corresponds to different formal fairness criteria (equal FPR and FNR together satisfies "Equality of Odds"; equal FNR alone satisfies "Equality of Opportunity"):

FPR = FPFP + TN     FNR = FNFN + TP // two of the four confusion-matrix metrics (with FDR and FOR) a model card is asked to report per subgroup and per subgroup intersection

Every reported value carries a 95% confidence interval from bootstrap resampling, following the probabilistic treatment of precision, recall, and F-score that Goutte and Gaussier established for exactly this purpose. For metrics that aren't confusion-matrix-based — the paper's own toxicity example uses Pinned AUC, a threshold-agnostic separability metric — the same disaggregation-by-Factor principle still applies; only the metric itself changes.

// what they found

The results

To make the abstract format concrete, the paper works two examples end to end. The first (Figure 2) is a smiling-face classifier — a CNN pretrained for face recognition and then fine-tuned for binary smile detection — evaluated on CelebA, disaggregated by the gender and age-group annotations CelebA already provides (young/old × male/female, plus their four intersections). At the 0.5 decision threshold, the card reports that all four error metrics — FPR, FNR, FDR, and FOR — "are within the same range (0.04–0.14)" across every subgroup and intersectional subgroup shown, with the plotted confidence intervals for old-male and young-male sitting toward the higher end of that band on several metrics. The card's own Caveats section is candid about what that comparison can't show: CelebA's annotations don't cover race or Fitzpatrick skin type — "which has been reported as a source of disproportionate errors" — and gender is treated as strictly binary ("male/not male").

The second worked example (Figure 3) documents Perspective API's public TOXICITY classifier, reported not with confusion-matrix rates but with Pinned AUC — the separability metric from Dixon et al.'s bias-in-text-classification work — measured per identity term (gay, lesbian, bisexual, straight, cis, trans, nonbinary, black, white, and their intersections) on a synthetic template-substitution test set built for exactly this purpose, chosen because real toxic-comment data is itself disproportionately about specific identity groups and would otherwise bias the evaluation. The card compares two shipped model versions side by side: version 1's per-group Pinned AUC visibly dips and spreads wider for some identity terms — bisexual and homosexual sit noticeably lower than straight and gay in the plotted bars — while version 5, after Perspective API's own bias-mitigation work, shows every group clustered tightly near the top of the scale. As the paper puts it, this is "consistent with what some users of the initial TOXICITY model found," and the comparison is a rare case of a model card literally documenting that a fix worked.

// does it hold up?

The eval read

Model Cards is a template paper, not an evaluated system, so there's no baseline to beat or benchmark to contaminate — the honest question is whether the standard it proposes actually gets filled in accurately and consistently, and the paper is unusually candid that it can't guarantee that. Its own Discussion states plainly: "It seems unlikely, at least in the near term, that model cards could be standardized or formalized to a degree needed to prevent misleading representations of model results (whether intended or unintended)." A model card is exactly as honest as whoever writes it, with no external verification built into the format — the same self-report gap Datasheets for Datasets has on the data side, which this paper cites directly as its methodological sibling.

The worked examples double as the paper's own stress test of that gap. The smiling-classifier card, offered as the flagship demonstration of disaggregated reporting, doesn't disaggregate by race or skin type at all — the exact axis Buolamwini and Gebru's Gender Shades (co-authored by this paper's own last author) had shown, months earlier, to carry the largest disparities in face-based classifiers. The Caveats section admits this outright rather than hiding it, but it means the demonstration proves the format can hold demographic breakdowns without actually forcing the hardest one to be reported — nothing in the template compels a card's author to disaggregate by the factor most likely to embarrass the model.

Adoption six-plus years out is real but uneven, and mostly voluntary. Hugging Face auto-generates a model-card stub for every uploaded checkpoint, which normalized the artifact's existence but not its rigor — most stubs never reach the disaggregated Quantitative Analyses this paper is actually about. The more rigorous end of the lineage looks different: OpenAI's GPT-4o System Card — also in this graph — folds in a Preparedness Framework risk scorecard and external red-teaming across 100+ testers in 45 languages plus third-party assessments from METR and Apollo Research, the kind of independent check the original Model Cards paper could only gesture toward wanting. But that's the frontier-lab exception, produced under external regulatory and reputational pressure that didn't exist in 2019 — it isn't evidence that the median released model, then or now, ships anything close to a complete, subgroup-disaggregated card.

baselines

None to speak of — this is a documentation template, not a model or metric competing against alternatives. Its closest prior art is Datasheets for Datasets (the data-side analogue) and the TRIPOD statement (the medicine-reporting analogue it explicitly cites), both used as design inspiration rather than benchmarks to beat.

contamination

Not applicable in the usual sense, but the format has an analogous integrity problem: nothing verifies that a card's reported metrics are accurate, complete, or computed the way the card claims — the same self-report weakness Datasheets for Datasets has on the data side.

what's missed

No enforcement or verification mechanism; no measurement of how many released models actually ship a card at all (the paper reports the format, not an adoption rate); and its own flagship example skips the single factor — race/skin type — that its own cited Gender Shades audit showed to matter most.

would raise confidence

A required or externally-audited subset of fields (the way GPT-4o's System Card folds in third-party red-teaming from METR and Apollo Research), a machine-readable card schema enabling automated compliance checks across a model registry, and a longitudinal measurement of what fraction of widely-deployed models ship a card at all — rather than the two hand-picked, well-behaved demonstration cases this paper shows.

// bottom line

Nearly 3,000 citations later, Model Cards succeeded as vocabulary and as a template — it's a direct ancestor of Hugging Face's model-card pages, Google's own Model Card Toolkit, and system cards like GPT-4o's — but it never became a floor. The paper predicted its own outcome almost exactly: it "seems unlikely" model cards could be formalized enough to stop misleading self-reporting, and six-plus years on, the honest, disaggregated card remains the exception that well-resourced or externally-pressured teams produce, not the default every released model carries.

// takeaways

What to remember

  1. Model cards standardize what a released model should disclose — model details, intended use, factors, metrics, evaluation/training data, quantitative analyses (unitary and intersectional), ethical considerations, caveats — modeled explicitly on the electronics industry's component datasheet and medicine's TRIPOD reporting statement.
  2. Its central methodological demand is disaggregation: report performance per demographic/environmental Factor and per intersection of Factors — not just in aggregate — using confusion-matrix metrics (FPR/FNR/FDR/FOR) or metrics like Pinned AUC, each with bootstrap confidence intervals, because a model can look fair overall while failing one subgroup badly.
  3. It's a template, not an audit: nothing verifies a card's claims, its own flagship example skips race/skin-type disaggregation despite citing Gender Shades as the reason that matters, and the paper itself doubted model cards alone could ever be formalized enough to stop misleading self-reporting.
